item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the catching on rock!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the closest source, here, shows no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"suspicious neon-like glow "

Researcher 1 " that glow…I don't like it…"
Researcher 2" yeaaahh… Looks like its upto something…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got a kick out of that "on rock" bit, and it's a very original idea. Conjures up some really surreal mental images. A well earned upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this had me absolutely sold until the explanation for not stuffing it in a box filled with noble gases was that it gave off a "neon glow" so what? its radioactive… duh something like this is more than likely radioactive.

no vote. needs a better reason than "it glows" to not be stuffed in a box filled wit argon

better yet, halon, the shit they douse gas stations in when they catch on fire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At one point (I can't find it now) you write that the stone maintains the same mass, yet gets heavier slowly. If mass is constant but weight is increasing, then local gravity is getting stronger, which I don't think is what you intended. I may be misreading it, but it confused me in either case, so maybe review the part about it getting in people's lungs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could use a bit of tidying up:

As a precaution, all subjects entering SCP-1814's chamber must undergo psych evaluation to detect pyromaniac tendencies. Such subjects are to be terminated.

Tested before they enter the chamber, or after? Is this a screening, or is the SCP suspected to actually trigger pyromaniac tendencies? Or would that be lithomaniac, in this case?

Throughout this process, SCP-1814-2 retains the same mass as an equal volume of pumice, gradually getting heavier.

This seems to contradict itself, unless SCP-1814-2 is expanding.

However, the concept is damn creative, the effects on human subjects are horrifying in the best tradition, and the overall article is well worth a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
without a note from a doctor

Are you serious?

Still, I love "catch on rock".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is one of the most creative and original SCP's I have seen, not to mention diabolically funny, +42

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I guess you could say the victims got…
*sunglasses*
TOTALLY STONED.
YEEEEEEEEEEEEEAAAAAAAH!

In all seriousness, this is great. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subjects eventually die from a combination of extreme pain, blunt force trauma, asphyxiation, and exfoliation.

Hell yes. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Catching on rock," while entertaining, seems like it might be tone-breaking in it's failure to appropriately describe the situation. It seems unlikely that the foundation would use the phrase "catching on fire," in most cases, and this bootlegged version doesn't seem like it should get a free pass for being cheeky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that probably, the phrase "catching on rock" is too ambiguous and informal to be included in the article proper. The first time, when they introduce the phrase, seems legitimate, but in excess of that, it seems like it's out of appropriate writing voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know it's been said, but catching people on rock is both the most amusing and the most horrifying thing I've read all day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, but the fact that you mention things "catching on rock" really just amuses me to no end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okie dokie!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's an awesome thought. Put it in the article proper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plus it's rock, so while it's burning from the inside, it's also scraping and piercing at your guts, until it erupts through the flesh of your torso. Not pretty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, it's perfectly fine, no apology needed. I do often come off as abstruse, so don't worry if you need me to explain something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't realize you were referencing something. I hadn't heard of the Denisovans before. I thought it was just a random detail thrown in to make it feel more mysterious. My apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just felt I needed to include an origin, and the Denisovans seemed reasonable. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My best guess is that since it's pumice, it would float in the water and reach the top of the tank, probably catching the tank on rock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, I had changed it earlier under some silly notion of expense, but no matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
agreed. if water put it out then that would be by FAR the easiest method of containment, keep it in a water tank and all would be well. it's so friggin obvious that the reader really ought to be calling out against it, hence why it NEEDS to be taken care of, with the best method being to make water make things worse somehow.

but storing in noble gases would definitely be easier than maintaining a vacuum chamber. you really might want to consider altering the containment procedures to that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aye, but that still doesn't explain why we couldn't use a non-combusting heavy gas, like the fire-control gasses in computer banks, or, I don't know, a noble gas. Or Nitrogen. Heck, Nitrogen or some other non-combusting gas at *high* pressure would be better, if there's no reason you can't use them: In the event of a container breach, it would take a while for oxygen to make its way into the chamber (Due to higher pressure in the chamber than out)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some typos, a few tonal issues, somewhat unnecessary expungement, and the detail about the undiscovered hominid subspecies just seems random and unneeded to me. However, the idea is excellently horrifying and the execution presented it well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just love this for the phrase "catch on rock". Suggestions: the sentence with the redaction in the containment procedures isn't necessary (it feels more like description, and containment redactions are frowned upon anyway), and 'betray' is a tone-breaking word. This is really good for a first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very good point and needs to be addressed. Not that water needs to put it out, but we need to know what HAPPENS if it is submerged in water, as water is the single most common method of putting out fires. Does it stop it? Slow it? No effect? Worsen it? Cause a horrible side effect?

I think the best thing I could recommend is that it separates the hydrogen and oxygen in water and then uses the oxygen to continue burning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You covered all possible rock analogs of the properties/actions of fire - each one was well considered and explored. Fun to read and painful to imagine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's a statue of fire, that actually acts like fire… The dust gets into your lungs (and presumably eyes) and burns you alive from the inside, while from the outside you're both burned and exfoliated to death.

Jesus Christ. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there a reason it can't be stored underwater? Or in a non-oxygenated gas, if it behaves like 'normal fire'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this needs a good copy-editing pass to get rid of some clunky language, but the object is fascinating. Upvoted, but still room for improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About the containment procedure: first, it would be a lot easier to keep the thing in a vacuum sealed box inside a normal chamber. It would be less effort to maintain, plus the box could be used to transport the SCP to and from testing. It could be made even simpler if the box was filled with water, which the SCP was immersed in, but that might cause problems with having to dry the thing off before testing.

Second:

This chamber must be must be free of any oxygen or other stray gases the only entrance being a double-seal airlock.

That sentence is redundant after saying the the chamber has to be in a vacuum sealed chamber.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which last line, the one in the addendum or the italicized quote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, except for the last line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but

D-class test subjects described this sensation as "like a belt sander" or "[EXPLETIVE]".

this line is not very descriptive and would be better removed (at least the [EXPLETIVE] part). It's just too cheeky.

That said, it's got that nice logical disconnect I like in SCPs. One impossible premise that leads to all sorts of interesting results.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that it reacts to gases, nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the effect of water or a fire extinguisher on the rockfire?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but that note seems a little silly. Regardless, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, this needs some tightening but I really like it. Good stuff. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree, that section was a little clunky.

Typos? Point them out, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: good work. could be tightened up a bit, but overall this is fairly solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The expungement hurts this a fair bit, and I have some style quibbles with how a few things are phrased, but in all it's a decent concept and not a bad execution.

EDIT: Oh hell, this is your first, isn't it? This is very good for a first attempt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This jar is to be contained in a standard Class-3 airtight containment chamber, the only entrance being a double-seal airlock. The jar must be free of any oxygen or other stray gases. The glass must be continually monitored for cracks, chips, or fractures. If any of these are noticed, the room must be immediately evacuated of gas, utilizing a large one-way air turbine in the ceiling above the jar, and the glass must be replaced by D-class subjects wearing pressurized HAZMAT suits and respirators.

Wouldn't it be easier to just keep the entire room a vacuum, since we've already got an airlock installed?

A few typos, but it's an interesting idea. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really cool. The Expungement at the end seems unnecessary though. It's not like we don't know what happens when the object is exposed to oxygen.
